name: Belgium Wave
description:
The Belgium Wave refers to a series of alleged UFO sightings that occurred primarily in Belgium between November 1989 and April 1990. It is considered one of the most extensively documented cases of UFO sightings in history. Proponents claim that thousands of people witnessed triangular-shaped objects moving silently at low altitude. These objects were often described as large, flat, and emitting little to no sound. Some reports also included claims of radar confirmation and photographic evidence. The Belgian Air Force even scrambled F-16 fighter jets on several occasions, though no direct visual contact or interception was made. 

Proponents argue that the sheer number of eyewitness accounts, coupled with radar data (though disputed), lends credibility to the extraterrestrial hypothesis. They highlight the consistency in descriptions across different locations and times. Some point to the lack of a conventional explanation for the observed maneuvers and shapes. However, mainstream skepticism points to the possibility of mass hysteria, misidentification of conventional aircraft (such as stealth aircraft being tested), and hoaxes. The official investigation concluded that the radar tracks could not be definitively linked to the reported visual sightings. Furthermore, many photographs and videos have been debunked as either misidentified objects or deliberate fabrications. The Belgian Air Force's scrambling of jets is often cited as proof of the severity of the situation, but skeptics argue it was a precautionary measure based on incomplete information and does not validate the existence of extraterrestrial craft. The alleged triangular shape is sometimes attributed to psychological expectations after widespread media coverage of similar UFO reports elsewhere. While the Belgium Wave remains a compelling case for some UFO enthusiasts, the mainstream scientific community largely attributes it to a combination of psychological, technological, and meteorological factors.
subtitle: Mass UFO sightings over Belgium.

claims.claim: Triangular UFOs were observed by thousands.
claims.criticism: Mass hysteria, misidentification of conventional aircraft, psychological expectations.

claims.evidence: Radar data allegedly showing anomalous movements and speeds.
claims.criticism: Radar malfunctions, misinterpretation of data, inability to correlate radar tracks with visual sightings.

claims.claim: Belgian Air Force scrambled fighter jets.
claims.evidence: Official record of F-16 scrambles during the wave.
claims.criticism: Precautionary measure based on incomplete information; no direct visual contact or interception achieved.

keyFigures.name: General Wilfried De Brouwer
keyFigures.role: Chief of Operations of the Belgian Air Force during the wave; authorized F-16 scrambles.

keyFigures.name: August Meessen
keyFigures.role: Physicist who investigated the events and promoted a skeptical viewpoint.

mythologyConnections: Possible connection to stories of aerial phenomena and 'flying shields' in folklore.

timeline.event: First significant wave of sightings begins.
Peak of the sightings; multiple reports and F-16 scrambles.
Debate and analysis of the Belgium Wave continue.

sources.author: Association d'Étude des Phénomènes Aérospatiaux (A.E.P.A.)
sources.work: Numerous reports and analyses of the Belgium Wave
sources.title: Association d'Étude des Phénomènes Aérospatiaux (A.E.P.A.)

sources.author: August Meessen
sources.work: Various articles and publications questioning the extraterrestrial hypothesis
sources.title: August Meessen

sources.author: Jacques Scornaux
sources.work: Vague OVNI sur la Belgique: Un Dossier Exceptionnel
sources.title: Jacques Scornaux
